summary: Only a second World Cup appearance (after three defeats in 1974). Sébastien Migné's side thrives on transitions and vertical football — patient in build-up, then sharply direct in attack, carrying the ball up and attacking the space behind with long passes.
style: 4-2-3-1 (Bellegarde behind the centre-forward) or 4-4-2 (two of Nazon, Pierrot, Isidor up top). Happy to pass it around in build-up, but vertical with passes and dribbles once they go forward — wingers in 1v1s, underlapping runs from full-back and midfield, quick transition attacks.

counterReliance.note: Patient build-up, then quick, vertical transition attacks

strengths: Dangerous, vertical transitions — carry the ball and attack the space behind
Genuine goal threat across three capable centre-forwards
weaknesses: Can leak goals — a point is likelier to come through scoring than a clean sheet

keyPlayers.name: Jean-Ricner Bellegarde
keyPlayers.note: France-youth product who switched allegiance in 2025; the key link between midfield and attack, ball-carrying in transition

keyPlayers.name: Duckens Nazon
keyPlayers.note: All-time top scorer (44 goals) and the focal point of the direct attack

keyPlayers.name: Wilson Isidor
keyPlayers.note: Committed to Haiti in March 2026; adds Premier League pedigree to the forward line

tactics: Build patiently, then break vertically at speed — isolate the wingers and run beyond the defence. The defensive lapses make them volatile, but the quick breaks are genuinely dangerous, and any point would spark celebrations in Port-au-Prince.
notes: Their only prior World Cup (1974) ended in three losses; a first-ever point is the goal.
sources: The Athletic — 2026 World Cup tactical guide, Group C (5 Jun 2026)
John Walker (@johnwalker_1986) — pre-tournament tactical scouting, May–Jun 2026
